reflection.quote: Freedom is what you do with what's been done to you.
reflection.reading:
Tomorrow is the Fourth. If you're new to this, the holiday weekend can feel like a countdown clock — the barbecues, the coolers, the fireworks somebody's uncle bought in a parking lot. The whole country seems to be pre-gaming, and you're wondering how you're supposed to sit through it sober without crawling out of your skin.

Here's what I wish someone had told me at 22: the freedom people are chasing with a case of Coors Light is a cheap counterfeit of the real thing. Real freedom is being able to drive yourself home. It's remembering the conversation you had on the porch. It's waking up on the fifth without a body count of texts to review or a knot in your stomach trying to reconstruct the night. Sartre's line hits because it refuses the easy version — freedom isn't the absence of hard things, it's what you make with the hand you were dealt.

Sobriety on a holiday isn't about white-knuckling through somebody else's party. It's about noticing that you already have the thing everyone's pretending to celebrate. Show up to the cookout. Eat too much. Leave when you want to leave. Watch the fireworks and actually see them. That's the whole assignment. One day, and this one happens to have hot dogs.
reflection.meditation: Where in my life am I mistaking escape for freedom?

affirmations: I can walk into any room this weekend without needing a drink to belong there.
Today I choose the version of myself I'll be glad to wake up as tomorrow.
I am building a life I don't need to check out of.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful today for a holiday weekend I get to actually remember. Grateful for the friends who know my story and the ones who don't need to. Grateful that the anxious kid I used to be on the Fourth — counting drinks, managing his buzz, dreading the comedown — doesn't have to run the show anymore.
gratitude.closing: One day at a time. Even this one. Especially this one.
